title: Instruction Tuning
lesson:
Instruction tuning is the step that turns a clever-but-clueless text predictor into something that actually follows your instructions. It is the difference between a model that mumbles plausible text and one that hears "Summarise this email in two sentences" and does exactly that.

Why a raw model ignores you

A fresh model fresh out of pretraining has only learned one game: guess the next word across a huge pile of internet text. That is it. So if you type "List three uses for a paperclip," it might happily continue with "List five uses for a rubber band, List ten uses for..." because on the internet, lines like that often come in clusters. It is not being difficult. It genuinely does not know that text-after-a-question is supposed to be an answer. It just completes patterns.

Think of a brilliant chef who has read every cookbook ever written but never worked in a kitchen with customers. Ask for "a quick pasta dish" and they might recite the history of pasta, or start a new recipe mid-sentence. The raw talent is all there. What is missing is the habit of treating a request as a request. Instruction tuning is the apprenticeship that builds that habit.

How it works: examples of being helpful

Instruction tuning is a flavour of fine-tuning done with a special kind of data: thousands of instruction → response pairs. Each example shows a task and a good completion of that task. We continue the same next-word-prediction training, but now every example teaches the pattern "when you see an instruction, produce a helpful answer." These collections are exactly what the SFT Datasets topic covers (SFT means supervised fine-tuning — "supervised" just means we show the model the correct answers).

A second analogy: it is like a new hire who knows the whole industry but not your office. You hand them a binder of "here's a typical request, here's how we handle it" examples. After enough of them, they internalise the style and start handling new requests the same way. They have not gained new facts so much as a new job description.

Crucially, the model generalises. Train on "translate, summarise, rewrite, explain" and it gets the broad idea of follow the instruction — so it can often handle a task you never put in the data. That generalisation is the whole magic.

What an example looks like

The data is usually structured so the model can tell which part is the human's instruction and which part is the reply it should learn to produce. A common format:

{
  "instruction": "Rewrite this sentence to be more polite.",
  "input": "Send me the report now.",
  "output": "Could you please send me the report when you get a chance?"
}

During training these fields get wrapped in a chat template (fixed marker text, like <|user|> and <|assistant|>, that labels who is speaking). The model only gets graded on predicting the output part — not the instruction. That detail matters: you want it to learn how to answer, not how to echo the question. See Dataset Formatting for how those templates are built.

Where it sits in the pipeline

The modern recipe for a chat model is roughly three stages: pretraining (learn language from raw text) → instruction tuning (learn to follow requests) → preference tuning (learn which answers humans actually like, via Preference Datasets and methods like DPO or RLHF). Instruction tuning is the bridge in the middle. It does not make the model nicer or safer on its own — it makes it obedient to the task shape. The polish comes later.

Base (pretrained) modelInstruction-tuned model
Continues text by patternAnswers the request
"List three uses" → more lists"List three uses" → three uses
Needs clever prompting to behaveBehaves with plain requests
Great raw material, awkward to useReady for chat-style products

In practice you rarely instruction-tune a giant model from scratch yourself. You either use an already-tuned model (most "-Instruct" or "-Chat" models you download are post-this-step), or you do a light instruction-tune on top of one using a cheap method like LoRA to teach your own task style. Either way, knowing this step exists explains the single most common beginner confusion: "why does the base model ignore my instructions?" Because nobody taught it that instructions were special yet.

💡 Rule of thumb: a few thousand high-quality, varied instruction examples beat a million repetitive ones. Diversity of tasks teaches generalisation; sheer volume of the same thing teaches a rut.
mentalModel: A genius chef who has read every cookbook but never served a customer. Instruction tuning is the apprenticeship that teaches one habit: when someone makes a request, treat it as a request and actually fulfil it.
mistakes: Confusing instruction tuning with teaching new facts. It mostly teaches behaviour — "answer the task." If you need fresh, up-to-date knowledge, reach for RAG or Continued Pretraining, not a handful of instruction pairs.

Grading the model on the whole sequence, including the instruction. You should mask (ignore) the instruction tokens and only train on the output. Otherwise the model wastes effort learning to reproduce questions instead of answering them.

Using a tiny, repetitive dataset. A thousand near-identical "summarise this" examples teaches one trick, not the general skill of following instructions. Variety of task types is what makes it generalise to unseen requests.

Forgetting the chat template the model expects. Each model family has its own marker format. Train or prompt with the wrong template and quality drops sharply, even though nothing looks broken. Match the template the base model was tuned with — see Dataset Formatting.
Expecting it to make the model safe or polite. Instruction tuning makes a model obey the task shape. Tone, refusals, and human preference are a separate later step (DPO / RLHF).

Over-training and causing catastrophic forgetting. Hammer too many epochs (full passes over the data) and the model can lose general ability while it overfits your narrow examples. Use few epochs and watch a held-out validation set.
exercise:
Grab a small base model (the non-instruct version, e.g. a 1B Llama or Qwen base) and a small instruction-tuned sibling of the same family. Give both the exact same plain prompt, like "Give me three tips for sleeping better.", with no clever prompt engineering. Write down both outputs side by side. You should clearly see the base model drift or ramble, while the instruct version answers directly.

Stretch goal: Build a tiny 30-example instruction dataset in the {instruction, input, output} format for one narrow task (say, turning messy notes into a clean bullet list), then LoRA-fine-tune the base model on it and re-run the same prompt to see the behaviour shift.
solution: Hint: The base model will often continue your prompt rather than answer it — repeating the question, inventing a follow-up question, or starting a list and never finishing it. The instruct model gives a clean, direct three-tip answer because it was shown thousands of "request → answer" examples. For the stretch goal: wrap each example in the model's chat template, mask the instruction tokens so the loss is computed only on output, train with LoRA for just 1–3 epochs, and keep the learning rate low. Even 30 examples will visibly nudge the style, though you need far more variety for real generalisation.
summary: Instruction tuning fine-tunes a raw text-predictor on thousands of instruction → response pairs so it learns the habit of actually doing what it is asked. It teaches behaviour, not facts, and it generalises to new tasks. It sits between pretraining and preference tuning, and it is why every -Instruct or -Chat model feels usable while a base model just rambles.

keyTerms.def: Fine-tuning a model on instruction-and-response examples so it learns to follow requests instead of just continuing text.
A model that has only been pretrained on raw text. It predicts the next word well but does not yet treat instructions as requests to answer.
Training a model by showing it correct input-output examples. 'Supervised' means the right answers are provided. Instruction tuning is a kind of SFT.
A single training example: a task (the instruction, sometimes with extra input) plus the desired answer (the output).
Fixed marker text that labels who is speaking (user vs assistant), wrapped around examples so the model can tell instruction from answer.
Ignoring the instruction tokens when scoring training, so the model is only graded on producing the answer, not on echoing the question.
The ability to handle a task that was never in the training data, learned by seeing many varied instruction types.
When over-training on narrow data makes a model lose abilities it previously had. Avoided with few epochs and a validation set.
One full pass over the entire training dataset. Instruction tuning usually needs only a small number of them.
